message.content:
Adversaries may backdoor web servers with web shells to establish persistent access to systems. A web shell is a web script placed on an accessible web server, allowing adversaries to use the server as a gateway into a network. Web shells can provide functions to execute commands or offer a command-line interface on the host system. Notable groups like APT28, APT29, and HAFNIUM have used web shells to maintain persistence, conduct lateral movement, and exfiltrate data. Commonly exploited servers include Microsoft Exchange and Internet Information Services (IIS).

The impact of web shells includes unauthorized access, data exfiltration, and potential further compromise of the network. Adversaries often use obfuscation and modified versions of known web shells to evade detection.

Mitigation strategies include regular patching of web servers, employing web application firewalls (WAFs), monitoring for unusual web server activity, and conducting regular security audits. Implementing strict access controls and using intrusion detection systems (IDS) can also help in identifying and mitigating web shell threats.
